title: How Trump Brought Non-Alignment Back From the Dead | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Trump's transactional, alliance-skeptical foreign policy is unwittingly adopting the substance of Cold War non-alignment/'strategic autonomy'; the piece argues history shows non-alignment is the weak's tool that leaves nations friendless, and that dismantling the post-1945 US alliance network sacrifices America's single greatest strategic asset.

insights.claim: A US administration that has threatened to pull out of NATO, declared all alliances transactional, and shown sympathy for Russia's territorial ambitions is embracing the substance of the Cold War non-alignment (today's 'strategic autonomy') doctrine it once derided.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Signals a structural, not merely rhetorical, US retreat from alliance guarantees—raising the risk premium on assets and regions that depend on US security backing.
insights.quote: America—which once derided the non-aligned movement and worked to undermine it—is increasingly, if unwittingly, adopting the core tenets of an ideology born in the capitals of Asia and Africa.
insights.locator: Non-aligned countries were, according to the U.S.

themes.theme: Non-alignment as realpolitik with hidden costs

insights.claim: Non-alignment was cold realpolitik, not morality: it let nations avoid binding alliances while extracting benefits from both superpowers—India took decades of development and military aid from both the US and USSR with scant obligations in return.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Frames strategic autonomy as free-riding that works only while a country is poor—useful lens for pricing which emerging powers can actually sustain fence-sitting.
insights.locator: It seemed a largely moral doctrine.

insights.claim: Non-alignment is the tool of the weak, not the strong, and has limited utility: as countries grow richer, benefits-without-obligations becomes unsustainable, and because alliances take years of shared trust to build, many non-aligned states ended the Cold War friendless.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Predicts that today's hedgers (and a hedging US) accrue delayed costs that surface as isolation when they most need allies.
insights.quote: it was the tool of the weak, not the strong
insights.locator: But the long history of non-alignment

insights.claim: China, which has entered no formal alliance since non-alignment's founding, has only highly transactional relationships even with partners like Russia, lacking the deep trust that characterized US-led alliances such as NATO.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Undercuts the thesis that a China-Russia axis is a durable bloc—implying it is brittle and opportunistic, relevant to geopolitical tail-risk pricing.
insights.locator: China, which has not entered a formal alliance

insights.claim: China distanced itself from Iran—one of its biggest oil suppliers and a major partner—during the US-led war, instead asking Tehran to be mindful of its Gulf neighbors' concerns.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Shows Beijing will not underwrite Iran in a crisis—dampening expectations that China provides Iran a strategic backstop during Gulf disruptions.
insights.locator: And although Iran is a major partner of China

insights.claim: India's non-alignment left it heavily dependent on Russian hardware and struggling to modernize its military despite trying to pivot to Western defense suppliers, and its tilt toward the Kremlin remains a lasting liability in the US-India partnership.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Flags a durable ceiling on US-India defense integration and a persistent opening for Western arms makers to displace Russian systems in India over time.
insights.locator: Non-alignment has even been problematic for India

themes.theme: Costs of US non-alignment already appearing

insights.claim: Trump's NATO-withdrawal musings, framing of Ukraine as Europe's problem, empathy for Putin's territorial claims, and Greenland annexation talk have driven allies to seek alternatives—Canada investing in Arctic military capabilities and Poland and Finland exploring bilateral defense pacts.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Points to a durable European/Arctic defense-spending and pact-formation wave independent of US procurement—watch for intra-European and bilateral defense deals.
insights.locator: The costs of non-alignment are already appearing

insights.claim: No US ally expressed—let alone provided—support or sympathy for US security interests in the Iran war, evidence that alliance erosion is already reducing burden-sharing when Washington needs it.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Concrete marker that US coalition-building capacity has degraded—relevant to how isolated future US military actions may be.
insights.quote: no ally expressed, let alone provided, any support or sympathy for US security interests in the Iran war
insights.locator: The costs of non-alignment are already appearing

insights.claim: The post-1945 network of trust, friendships, and shared obligations is potentially the single greatest strategic asset the US possesses, and dismantling it for flexibility misses the core lesson of non-alignment's failures.
insights.speaker: Manjari Chatterjee Miller
insights.so_what: Core investable thesis: US soft-power/alliance capital is being spent down, a slow-burn geopolitical negative that compounds over years.
insights.quote: That network could be the single greatest strategic asset the US possesses.
insights.locator: Non-aligned countries never managed to build
